summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – DE FACTO – Existence of de facto relationship – Where the applicant claims the parties were in a de facto relationship for 25 years – Where the respondent seeks a declaration pursuant to s 90RD of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) that a de facto relationship never existed – Where the respondent claims the parties only had a close personal friendship – Consideration of the nature of the relationship – Consideration of the credit of the witnesses – Where the applicant fails to establish a de facto relationship existed within the meaning of s 4AA of the Act – Declaration made pursuant to s 90RD that a de facto relationship never existed. FAMILY LAW – DE FACTO – PROPERTY – Where the applicant sought property adjustment under s 90SM – Where the Court was not satisfied of the existence of a de facto relationship – Where the Court’s jurisdiction was not attracted under Pt VIIIAB – Where even if the Court’s jurisdiction was attracted, the Court was not satisfied it would be just and equitable to make any order altering the property of the parties – Application dismissed.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARPER J:

INTRODUCTION

These are proceedings under Pt VIIIAB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) between the applicant, Mr Braun (“applicant”) and the respondent, Mr Sanchez (“respondent”).
The applicant contends the parties were in a de facto relationship for approximately 25 years, commencing in 1998, and continuing until it broke down in October 2023. He does not seek a declaration to this effect, but claims orders altering the property interests of the parties pursuant to s 90SM of the Act. The respondent denies a de facto relationship ever existed, and seeks a corresponding declaration under s 90RD of the Act with dismissal of the applicant’s application.
The Court will only have jurisdiction to alter property interests if it is satisfied that any de facto relationship between the parties lasted for a period of at least two years (s 90SB(a)). The declaration sought by the respondent, if made, will deprive the Court of jurisdiction under Pt VIIIAB.
In the event the de facto issue is determined in the applicant’s favour, the applicant claims an assessment of his contributions at 40 per cent with no adjustment sought for future needs, and consequent property alteration orders under s 90SM to effect a 40/60 division in favour of the respondent.
The respondent seeks a declaration under s 90RD that the applicant did not make any substantial contributions of the kind contained in ss 90SM(4)(a), 90SM4(b) and 90SM4(c). He claimed it was not just and equitable for there to be any property adjustment and sought the applicant’s application be dismissed.
The composition of the joint balance sheet was agreed with a net asset pool of $8,061,001 (Exhibit H).
There are no children of the relationship.
Neither party was a good witness. I did not find the applicant to be reliable. I accept English is not his first language. Some allowance must be made for this, even if he did not require an interpreter. However, he displayed a good facility with English in the witness box. A comparison of his oral evidence with the intrinsic merit or demerit in the overall evidence and known facts undermined his credit (Wei & Xia (No 5) [2023] FedCFamC1F 679; (2023) 67 Fam LR 421 at [179]–[180]). I formed the view that the applicant had a tendency to embellish aspects of his case. To take one example, he claimed in his trial affidavit that he had a level of involvement in liaising with tenants at B Street, Suburb C, owned by the respondent, which he conceded in cross examination was exaggerated. Other deficiencies in his evidence will be discussed in the course of these reasons. He was argumentative with counsel for the respondent in cross examination, and often tried to answer a question with a question of his own. He frequently used answers to questions as an opportunity to advocate for his case as opposed to simply answering the question.
For his part, the respondent answered questions more directly than the applicant. However, he also presented in certain problematic ways. He frequently talked over the questioner, was argumentative and failed to make concessions. However, despite these aspects of his oral evidence, overall I formed the view that his evidence was more reliable than the evidence of the applicant, and it accorded more consistently with the documentary evidence and the inherent probabilities. I generally prefer the evidence of the respondent, unless otherwise stated in these reasons.

NARRATIVE

The following is a narrative of key factual matters. Unless otherwise stated, these facts are either undisputed or constitute findings of fact where necessary.
The applicant was born in 1979 in City D, Country E and is currently 46 years of age. He is employed in the education industry.
The respondent was born in 1951 and is currently 74 years of age. He is retired. He previously worked as in the education industry and owned a business, called “F Company” in Sydney, from 1988 until the early 2000s.
In 1992, the respondent purchased the Suburb C property for $580,000, funded by his personal savings and a mortgage with Westpac for $251,000. A second mortgage was later obtained to secure $41,000 as a business loan for the business. The loan was repaid by 1998, but the mortgage was not discharged. In 2010, a further mortgage was obtained with the Commonwealth Bank for $1,000,000. The funds were used to repay $80,455 left owing on the Westpac mortgage and to fund renovations to the Suburb C property. The Commonwealth Bank mortgage was serviced by the respondent and has been repaid, though not formally discharged. The respondent continues to have available lines of credit with the Commonwealth Bank for up to $1,000,000, which as at the date of trial has been drawn down on for about $110,000 mostly to pay legal fees. The line of credit has a 6 per cent interest rate and is serviced by the respondent from the rent he receives from tenants. The applicant made no financial contributions to the purchase of the Suburb C property.
In late 1997, at the age of 18 years, the applicant immigrated to Australia on a student visa. He had no assets of significance at the time. He studied a bachelor’s degree at G University and lived in student dormitory accommodation.
The parties’ account of their meeting and first date are largely the same, the differences minor. It appears the parties met online, chatted for some time before meeting in person at Museum Station in April or May 1998, according to the applicant or December 1998 according to the respondent. The respondent conceded he initially lied about his age to the applicant, stating he was 36 years rather than 47 years of age, because he did not think they would stay in contact, but later told the applicant his correct age. The parties agree they had sexual intercourse on their first date and the applicant stayed overnight at the respondent’s home in Suburb C.
From 1998, the applicant claimed he spent four to six nights per week at the respondent’s Suburb C home and the balance of the week at his student accommodation. He suggested he maintained the student accommodation because his family paid for it and that he was uncomfortable disclosing his living situation to his family because he was not openly gay. He travelled between the two locations by public transport, which took up to 45 minutes. He claimed he kept toiletries and some personal belongings at the Suburb C property, and that the respondent introduced him to his friends as his “boyfriend”. According to the applicant, within weeks of meeting, he considered the parties to be in a de facto relationship.
The respondent argued that between 1998 to 2002, the applicant would stay overnight at the Suburb C property no more than two nights per week, and not every week. He denied the applicant kept personal belongings at his home in the early period of their relationship. He claimed he only visited the applicant's student accommodation on one occasion, and that he told the applicant, “I am not looking for a serious relationship. But I would like to see you again” (respondent’s affidavit filed 8 December 2025, paragraph 18). Thereafter he contended, for the next 25 years, the parties were in a “close personal friendship”. The respondent claimed he did not expect, nor intend, for the relationship to be permanent or exclusive. Clearly, at least early after meeting, the parties had different perceptions of their level of commitment. The respondent may have referred to the applicant as his “boyfriend” on occasion, but I am not persuaded he did so habitually or consistently in the early years of the relationship.
The parties agreed that after about three months of dating, the respondent gave the applicant a key to his Suburb C property. The applicant claimed in cross examination he received a key in March 1999, and that from this date he considered himself to be living with the respondent in the Suburb C property. This is inconsistent with the applicant’s contention the parties commenced dating around April or May 1998, but accords with the respondent’s account of the parties meeting in December 1998. It was agreed the respondent gave the applicant a key so he could come and go from the Suburb C property without needing the respondent’s express permission. The applicant claimed he gradually began cooking and taking on household chores as he continued spending time at the Suburb C property. The respondent denied the applicant did household chores at the Suburb C property, save for occasionally cooking or washing dishes.
In mid-1998, the applicant contends the respondent introduced him to his family as “my friend” at his birthday celebrations. In cross examination he claimed that when the respondent started to introduce him to his family members was when he no longer considered the parties to be in a casual sexual relationship. He claimed the respondent thereafter regularly invited him to spend time with his family, sometimes these visits would involve overnight stays. The respondent claimed the parties themselves did not even meet until December 1998, and that the applicant did not meet his family until Christmas in 1999. He stated that at no time has his family referred to the applicant as his “partner” (respondent’s affidavit filed 8 December 2025, paragraph 206(l)), but they referred to him as his “friend”. He agreed the applicant spent time with his family on occasion. I accept the respondent’s recollection is more accurate in this regard.
In December 1998, and every December thereafter to separation, the applicant claims the parties spent Christmas together. The respondent agreed the parties spent Christmas together in 1999, and each year thereafter including Christmas 2023, which was after the applicant argued the relationship had broken down. He accepted that a number of times they stayed at his mother’s house for a number of consecutive days over the Christmas period. Sometimes the parties would host the respondent’s family for celebrations at the Suburb C property.
At the time the parties commenced a relationship, the respondent owned a property at H Street, Suburb J purchased by him in 1980.
In 1999, the applicant transferred between bachelor’s degrees.
In or around 1999 or 2000, the applicant claimed the respondent introduced him as his “partner” to his friends, Mr and Mrs TT. The respondent denied this occurred. No evidence was provided by Mr TT or Mrs TT. Again, I prefer the evidence of the respondent in this regard.
In 2001, the respondent sold his business for approximately $20,000, with the proceeds used to purchase a car. He then enrolled in a Graduate Diploma at the UU University to become an educator. In 2002, the respondent graduated from UU University and was employed as an educator from 2003 until 2018 at VV School. The applicant claimed this occurred in or around 2008, but agreed in cross examination he was wrong.
The applicant asserted the respondent studied for his diploma in 2006 in his trial affidavit. In cross examination he suggested it may be 2007 or 2008. The applicant claims he prepared meals, remained at the Suburb C and did extra housework to help support the respondent while he was studying. However, this evidence was not compelling. As I understood it, he claimed he provided this assistance in 2006, well after the respondent had graduated. In the period, 2001 to 2002, the applicant was studying at the G University, graduating with a Bachelor’s Degree in 2002. By 2006 he was at work with Q Company during the hours he claimed the respondent was studying. By 2006, the respondent had already completed his studies. The respondent denied the applicant did household chores for him and states he used his rental income from the Suburb J property to support himself while studying. I prefer the evidence of the respondent.
In 2002, the applicant graduated from his tertiary studies and his student accommodation terminated. He states he did not consider moving into the respondent’s Suburb C property full time because he was hiding his sexuality from his family and keeping the relationship private. He claims he needed separate accommodation to “keep up the façade” (applicant’s affidavit filed 24 November 2025, paragraph 51). The applicant moved into a rental apartment at K Street, Suburb L with his brother, Mr M, but continued to spend four to six nights per week at the respondent’s home. The respondent claimed that from 2003 the applicant spent about three nights per week at the Suburb C property. The respondent claimed he did not spend any time at the Suburb L apartment, and did not contribute to the rent. This was not in dispute. The applicant claimed his other living expenses were paid by the respondent. The respondent denied this and contended apart from paying for the occasional meal or movie ticket, the applicant’s living expenses were paid by his brother or parents. I accept the evidence of the respondent as more plausible.
In late 2002, the applicant lodged an application for a Subclass 880 (Skilled) Visa as an independent overseas student. Throughout the application, he gave the Suburb L property as his permanent address. He gave the same address in his curriculum vitae prepared for inclusion in the application (Exhibit C). Where the application form asked the applicant to specify his relationship status, he ticked “Never married” even though the form specifically provided for “de facto relationship” as an option (Exhibit D), which he did not nominate. This is despite claiming he and the respondent had been in a de facto relationship for about four years at this time.
According to his trial affidavit, in or around 2002 to 2003, the applicant commenced working as a casual in the fast-food industry at O Restaurant in Suburb N. In his oral evidence he said it was his first job in Australia and he worked about one day per week. However, the employment history in his visa application and curriculum vitae stated he commenced employment with O Restaurant in December 1998 until 2000.
In 2005, the applicant graduated with a Diploma at P College.
On 24 September 2005, the applicant commenced full time work in customer service with Q Company in Suburb R. He was initially paid a salary of $33,500 per annum, exclusive of superannuation. He was promoted and remained in the role until 2017. Through his employment he had access to clothing items that were faulty or returned by customers, which he received for free or purchased at a discounted price. The applicant gave these items, including a number of polo shirts, to the respondent and members of his family. The respondent denies the applicant’s claims that his salary led him to make financial contributions towards the respondent’s household expenses or similar.
From around 2008, the parties attended events hosted by the social group known as S Club. The events included yum-cha lunches, beach picnics and golf. The applicant claimed they attended events together and through the group made mutual friends.
In mid-2009, the applicant and his brother, Mr M, purchased a property as tenants in common in equal shares at T Street, Suburb U for $608,500. The applicant and Mr M each paid $30,425 towards the deposit, and the balance of $557,107 was secured by mortgage with V Bank. The applicant claimed his mother also contributed in some amount to the purchase deposit. The applicant and Mr M equally service the mortgage repayments and property expenses. The applicant and his brother intended for their mother to live in the property.
The applicant accepted in cross examination that in obtaining a mortgage with V Bank to purchase the Suburb U property and at all times in dealings with banks, he did not and has not used the Suburb C property as his address. He accepted that as far as the banks are concerned, he did not live at the Suburb C property or use the address to receive notices.
There was no dispute the respondent made no financial contribution to the purchase of the Suburb U property. The applicant claimed he had a prior discussion with the respondent about the purchase of the property and showed him some brochures. The respondent denied there was any discussion, and claimed he was not aware of either the applicant’s intention to purchase property or the acquisition of the Suburb U property until after its purchase by the applicant. I accept the evidence of the respondent in this regard.
After purchase, the applicant and Mr M moved into the Suburb U property, but the applicant continued to spend time at the Suburb C property. It was a common position that from 2009 the applicant stayed four nights per week at the Suburb C property, being Wednesday, and Friday to Sunday evenings. The applicant suggested in cross examination he might have stayed up to five nights per week until 2012, whilst the respondent claimed the parties often went camping or sailing together on the weekends so at times the applicant only spent about one night per week at the Suburb C property. I accept the respondent’s evidence as more accurate. The applicant would travel between Suburb C to his job in Suburb R, despite Suburb U being closer and more convenient to his workplace.
In late 2009, the applicant applied for a Subclass 143 (Contributory Parent) visa to allow his mother to immigrate to Australia. To finalise the application, in 2011 the applicant was required to provide to Centrelink a bank guarantee of $10,000 as an Assurance of Support for his mother. He gave the Suburb U property as his address to Centrelink. He also had to pay agent’s fees though he could not recall the amount. The visa application was approved in 2011.
In 2009, the applicant claims the respondent introduced him as his “partner” to his friend, Mr W, and told Mr W they “have been together for 11 years now” (applicant’s affidavit filed 24 November 2025, paragraph 97). The respondent denies this occurred. Mr W did not give evidence. I accept the respondent’s evidence as more accurate.
In or around 2010, the applicant claims the respondent stayed overnight at the Suburb U property and that he told his brother Mr M, “[the respondent] is my long term partner” (applicant’s affidavit filed 24 November 2025, paragraph 95). The applicant claims his brother “berated” him for being in a same sex relationship with an older man (applicant’s affidavit filed 24 November 2025, paragraph 95).
In October 2010, the applicant applied for Australian citizenship. He gave the Suburb U property as his address in this application. The respondent vouched for the applicant’s identity in the application. The applicant made a declaration in the form that all the information supplied was “complete, truthful and correct in every detail” (Exhibit F).
In late 2010, the respondent sold the Suburb J property for $954,000. He had leased and received rental income from the Suburb J property between 1992 to 2010.
At some point during 2010, the respondent commenced plans for extensive renovations to the Suburb C property. The renovation was extensive and resulted in four rooms being available for rent to lodgers. He gave evidence that he managed the project and met all costs of the renovations from the Commonwealth Bank mortgage and the sale proceeds of the Suburb J property. The construction took about two years to complete, during which period the respondent continued living at the Suburb C property.
The applicant claimed he provided manual labour during the renovations to help the respondent, without remuneration. He claims he swept up construction debris for easy disposal, moved pot plants, removed tree stumps and branches, moved furniture around, and crawled under the house with the respondent to move bricks. The applicant claimed the respondent stayed a few nights at the Suburb U property during renovations.
The respondent denies the applicant’s claims of contributing manual labour to the renovations. His evidence was that he dealt with the architect, builder and trades people, chose paint colours and fittings, bathroom vanities, tiles, fireplace, wardrobes and kitchen cupboards. He claimed the applicant had no involvement in the renovation and he did not ask him for his opinion on any items selected by the respondent. He denied staying at the Suburb U property and claimed the applicant did not offer for him to stay there during renovations. I accept the respondent’s evidence in this regard.
Upon completion of renovations, about four bedrooms of the property were tenanted from around 2012 up until 2023, and are presently tenanted. The rooms were rented for $200 to $320 per week, paid directly to the respondent in cash. According to the respondent, he uses the cash to pay for his utilities, groceries and living expenses, or deposits it with the Commonwealth Bank. The respondent claims that he managed all aspects of the tenancy and that the applicant had no involvement. The applicant argued that the parties together arranged for the rooms to be tenanted, and that over the years he liaised with the tenants and helped manage them in various ways, including collecting rent if the respondent was away. The respondent denied any such level of involvement by the applicant. There was little evidence to support the applicant’s version. One tenant, Mr X, gave evidence of one occasion when he tried to give the applicant rent when the respondent was away, but the applicant told him to take it up with the respondent on his return. The applicant conceded the respondent prevented him from replying to emails and interacting with prospective tenants who wanted to inspect the property. I accept the version of the respondent in this regard where it differs from the applicant.
In early 2011, the applicant was granted Australian citizenship.
In 2012, the applicant’s mother immigrated to Australia and moved into the Suburb U property with the applicant and Mr M. The mother had been diagnosed with a heart condition and required care from the applicant and his brother.
The applicant stayed with his mother at the Suburb U property on Monday, Tuesday and Thursday each week, and stayed with the respondent at the Suburb C property on Wednesday, Friday, Saturday and Sunday.
At this time in 2012, the applicant told his mother that he was in a same sex relationship with the respondent. He had previously told his friends, some colleagues, his brother, uncle and aunt.
In 2013, the applicant’s mother met the respondent and he claimed the three of them often went out for lunch and dinner. The respondent said he spent time with the applicant’s mother no more than twice per month, which I accept is more likely to be correct.
Throughout the years, the parties took many holidays together, often road trips to camping locations within Australia.
The respondent also travelled alone or with friends without the applicant at times. There was no dispute that the respondent sometimes went sailing by himself. On one occasion the respondent went on holiday to Country EE with a friend without the applicant. There was some disagreement about the date of the trip, but it was put to the applicant in cross examination that the trip occurred in 2013, which he accepted. According to the applicant, during the holiday a sexual encounter of some form occurred between the respondent and a masseuse. The applicant claims he was “shocked” and “hurt” that the respondent had “strayed” in their otherwise committed relationship. The respondent denies the encounter occurred, I accept it is more likely than not.
From around 2015, the parties attended monthly dinner events hosted at the Y Club. The applicant claimed they attended as a couple. Sometimes they arrived at the venue together, other times not. The respondent denied the pair socialised or showed affection to each other during the dinners, instead each spent time with their respective friends, and paid for their own meals. After such events, the parties stayed overnight at the Suburb C property.
In his 2015 tax return the applicant denied he had a spouse “married or de facto” in the financial year 1 July 2014 to 30 June 2015 (Exhibit I).
In or around 2016, the parties attended monthly walks with a social bushwalking group. The applicant described the group as a “LGBTQI bushwalking group of mixed couples” (applicant’s affidavit filed 28 November 2025, paragraph 49). He claimed the respondent introduced him as his “partner” but admitted in cross examination that single people and friends also attended. The respondent claimed he only referred to the applicant as his “friend” or by name to the group. The parties travelled to and from the meet ups together and would stay overnight at the respondent’s home afterwards.
The parties also attended an annual costume ball and disco in the Region Z between the years of 2016 up and until 2023. The balls were hosted by an LGBT group. The parties travelled together and often stayed several nights with mutual friends. They paid for their own tickets, or were reimbursed, and shared costs of accommodation. The respondent claims he did not refer to the applicant as his “partner” or “boyfriend” at the ball.
In 2017, the applicant was forced to resign from his employment at Q Company on account of performance. He undertook a part-time online course with AA University, which he completed on 11 September 2020. Throughout his studies he worked as a tutor for a couple of hours, two or three days per week at BB Company in Suburb L; and as an Educator at CC Company in Suburb DD for several hours a couple days per week.
In late 2017, the applicant applied to renew his NSW Driver’s Licence. He again gave his address as the Suburb U property. As at the date of trial, his driver’s licence address continues to be the Suburb U property.
In 2018, the respondent retired from his employment as an educator.
In the same year, the applicant continued to present the Suburb U property as his address to receive statements from his superannuation fund, Super Fund 1. The applicant conceded in cross examination that he never nominated the respondent as his beneficiary upon death. He agreed he nominated the Suburb U property as his address in 2018 despite claiming he believed the Suburb C property was the parties’ “forever home”. He then made the startling concession that he “now”, during the proceedings, tells his superannuation fund that the Suburb C property is his address, even though, on his own case, the relationship broke down in October 2023.
In late 2020, the applicant applied for a Working With Children Check in which he gave his address as the Suburb U property. The Application and Informed Consent Form provided by the applicant to the NSW Department of Education in September 2020 for a Nationally Co-ordinated Criminal History Check recorded his address as the Suburb U property. This latter form contained an acknowledgement by the applicant that he had “fully and accurately” completed it, and the information he provided in it could be used by other regulatory bodies.
In 2020, the applicant was employed as a part time casual educator at FF School. He later became accredited as an educator. He claims he applied for the job to be closer to the respondent and Suburb C.
In May 2022, the respondent’s mother, Mrs GG, moved into the Suburb C property on account of ill health. She is bed bound and is currently 99 years of age. The applicant claims he provided personal care for her on the four nights per week he stayed at the property. The respondent denied the applicant was involved in any significant care of his mother. He argued his mother received daily professional care and that he was otherwise responsible for her needs. Mrs GG gave oral evidence from her bed electronically. Despite her advanced age she gave evidence firmly and clearly. She denied the applicant’s claims concerning her care. I accept her evidence.
In 2023, the applicant’s mother died. The respondent attended the small family funeral. The applicant inherited $244,638, of which $20,000 was applied to purchase shares, $150,000 was put into a term deposit, and the balance went to discharge the applicant’s share of the mortgage on the Suburb U property.
In October 2023, the parties separated. This is common ground. It was the applicant’s case that the alleged de facto relationship broke down and ended at this point. The applicant says he removed his belongings from the Suburb C property. The respondent denies the applicant had any belongings to be removed.
Between October 2023 to March 2024, the applicant claims he continued to visit the Suburb C property at least four days per week. He continued to have a key to the property. He would often stay overnight and the parties continued a sexual relationship. The applicant claims he continued to care for the respondent’s mother during his visits. The parties continued to attend social dinners and activities such as bushwalks. They spent Christmas together in December 2023. The respondent gave the applicant a birthday card in 2024 that contained a printed birthday wish and the respondent added in handwriting “and lots more to come, Love [Mr Sanchez]”.
From May 2024, the applicant claimed the respondent no longer wanted to communicate or spend time with him. At this point in time the parties stopped going to social events and other activities such as dinners, bushwalks and theatre. The respondent claimed the applicant told him he was in a committed relationship with another man and he understood the applicant to not want to continue his friendship with the respondent.
On 6 November 2024, the applicant returned his key to the Suburb C property to the respondent. The applicant has not attended the Suburb C property since.
As at 1 September 2025, the applicant’s address on the Australian Electoral Roll was the Suburb U property, and remains his address as at the date of trial.

PROCEDURAL HISTORY

Some procedural history is relevant.
On 31 January 2025, the applicant commenced proceedings by filing an Initiating Application in Division 2 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia. He sought property orders to effect a 50/50 division of the parties’ assets.
On 24 March 2025, the respondent filed a Response to Initiating Application seeking a declaration that no de facto relationship existed and dismissal of the applicant’s application.
On 27 June 2025, the applicant filed an Amended Initiating Application seeking property division 40/60 in favour of the respondent.
On 4 July 2025, procedural orders were made that included a notation that the parties agreed the threshold issue pursuant to s 90RD and the issue as to whether there ought to be any alteration of property interests pursuant to s 90SM, be heard and determined concurrently.
The final hearing commenced on 13 January 2026 and ran for four days with judgment reserved on 16 January 2026.

MATERIAL RELIED UPON

The applicant relied upon the following documents:
(a) Case Outline filed on 5 January 2026;

(b) Further Amended Initiating Application filed 26 September 2025;

(c) Points of Claim filed 21 May 2025;

(d) Affidavit of applicant filed 24 November 2025;

(e) Affidavit in reply of applicant filed 15 December 2025;

(f) Affidavit of Mr M filed 24 November 2025;

(g) Affidavit of Mr HH filed 24 November 2025;

(h) Affidavit of Mr JJ filed 24 November 2025;

(i) Affidavit of Mr KK filed 24 November 2025;

(j) Affidavit of Mr LL filed 24 November 2025;

(k) Financial Questionnaire filed 31 January 2025; and

(l) Financial Statement filed 24 November 2025.

The applicant was cross examined.
Mr JJ was cross examined.
Mr HH was cross examined.
Mr M was cross examined.
The respondent relied upon the following documents:
(a) Case Outline filed 8 January 2026;

(b) Amended Response to Initiating Application filed 31 July 2025;

(c) Defence to Points of Claim filed 20 June 2025;

(d) Affidavit of respondent filed 8 December 2025;

(e) Affidavit of Mrs GG filed 8 December 2025;

(f) Affidavit of Mr X filed 8 December 2025;

(g) Affidavit of Mr MM filed 8 December 2025;

(h) Affidavit of Ms NN filed 8 December 2025;

(i) Financial Statement filed 8 December 2025; and

(j) Undertaking as to Disclosure filed 8 December 2025.

The respondent was cross examined.
Mrs GG was cross examined.
Mr X was cross examined.
Ms NN was cross examined.
The documents tendered and received into evidence are set out in Schedule 1 to these reasons.

Expert evidence

There was no expert evidence in this matter.

DE FACTO RELATIONSHIP

Determination of the existence and timing of a de facto relationship is an evaluative factual decision (Lennon & Sanil [2020] FamCAFC 109; (2020) FLC 93-962 at [8]), establishing the jurisdictional facts to enliven the discretionary power to adjust the parties’ property interests under Pt VIIIAB (Corporation of the City of Enfield v Development Assessment Commission (2000) 199 CLR 135 at [28]). It is not a discretionary decision.
In order for the Court to have the jurisdiction to make property orders under Pt VIIIAB the evidence must also establish the parties had a de facto relationship for a period of two years or more (s 90SB of the Act).
Section 4AA of the Act provides that a person is in a de facto relationship if three criteria are satisfied:
(1) A person is in a de facto relationship with another person if:
(a) the persons are not legally married to each other; and

(b) the persons are not related by family (see subsection (6)); and
(c) having regard to all the circumstances of their relationship, they have a relationship as a couple living together on a genuine domestic basis.
There was no dispute the first two criteria were satisfied. The debate concerned s 4AA(1)(c).
The applicant accepted that he bears the onus of establishing the parties, having regard to all the circumstances, were “living together on a genuine domestic basis” for a period of two years or longer. The respondent however also carries an onus to justify his proposed declaration pursuant to s 90RD.
Section 4AA(2) sets out a range of factors to which the Court may have regard for the purposes of s 4AA(1)(c):
(2) Those circumstances may include any or all of the following:
(a) the duration of the relationship;

(b) the nature and extent of their common residence;

(c) whether a sexual relationship exists;
(d) the degree of financial dependence or interdependence, and any arrangements for financial support, between them;

(e) the ownership, use and acquisition of their property;

(f) the degree of mutual commitment to a shared life;
(g) whether the relationship is or was registered under a prescribed law of a State or Territory as a prescribed kind of relationship;

(h) the care and support of children;

(i) the reputation and public aspects of the relationship.
No one matter set forth in s 4AA(2) must be found as a fact to establish a de facto relationship, nor does any one factor take precedence (s 4AA(3)). It is not essential that all the matters in s 4AA(2) are established. The Court has regard to all of the circumstances, to such matters, and to attach such weight to any matter, as may seem appropriate (s 4AA(4)).
It is worth observing that the various circumstances set out in s 4AA(2) may overlap, or inform each other. For example, the nature and extent of a common residence may inform the evaluation of the degree of mutual commitment to a shared life and vice versa. It has long been recognised that each element of a relationship draws its colour and its significance from the other elements, some of which may point in one direction and some in the other (Lynam v Director-General of Social Security [1983] FCA 274; (1984) FLC 91-577 at 79,663; Sinclair & Whittaker [2013] FamCAFC 129; (2013) FLC 93-551 (“Sinclair & Whittaker”) at [51]–[56]).
The High Court has explained the broad evaluative approach which the Court should take: “Consistently with the reality that human relationships are infinitely mutable, in determining whether a de facto relationship exists a court is entitled to have regard to such matters, and to attach such weight to any matter, as may seem appropriate” (Fairbairn v Radecki [2022] HCA 18; (2022) 275 CLR 400 (“Fairbairn”) at [28]). In a given case, some of the factors listed in s 4AA(2) may be relevant and some may be irrelevant; inevitably some may have greater prominence than others (Fairbairn at [39]).
The composite expression in s 4AA(1)(c) has received much judicial attention. The focus is on the “nature and quality” of the relationship, rather than a quantification of time spent together (Jonah & White (2012) FLC 93-522 at 86,683). The Court must look at the “composite picture” of the relationship (Sinclair & Whittaker at [54]–[55]). The gist of the inquiry is the degree to which parties have merged their lives into one, which connotes financial, emotional and physical interdependence (Rooks & Padley [2014] FamCA 444 (“Rooks & Padley”) at [10]).
Hallen J in Bezjak v Wyatt [2018] NSWSC 199 at [63] described the determination of the question of whether a de facto relationship exists as:
... essentially impressionistic. Such a relationship only exists because of the factual circumstances of the parties, unlike marriage, where there is a legal status immediately created at the time of the public ceremony and registration. A value judgment is required to be formed. Accordingly, the Court is often required to assess multiple pieces of circumstantial evidence. If there are sufficient pieces of evidence, when viewed, with care and sensitivity, cumulatively, and with common sense and proper reasoning, which satisfy the finder of fact that the relationship is a de facto relationship, then the statutory test is met ...
Hallen J continued at [69]:
... the concept of a de facto relationship is complex and diverse. Such a relationship can, and should, be distinguished from the relationship of two people, who live apart, but who have a sexual relationship, and who sleep over at one another’s house; and also from the relationship of two people, who share a house, but who do not have a commitment to each other, and who have other sexual partners. The fact that one provides the other, on occasions, with financial, or other, assistance, such as accommodation, to alleviate hardship does not mean that they are in a de facto relationship. In this way, a de facto relationship is different from friendship, or courtship, which has not matured into the commitment where there is a merging of lives so that there is a mutual commitment to a shared life. It is also different from simple companionship.
It has been held that “domestic basis” requires a level of domesticity, “which refers to home conditions and arrangements” (Taisha & Peng [2012] FamCA 385; (2012) 48 Fam LR 150 at [20]).
In relation to the concept of “living together”, in Fairbairn the High Court held:
Living together for the purposes of s 4AA(1) will often, perhaps usually, mean cohabitation of some residence by a couple for some period of time. But cohabitation of a residence or residences is not a necessary feature of “living together”. That phrase must be construed to take account of the many various ways in which two people may share their lives together in the modern world. Two people, for any number of reasons, may not reside in the same residence, but nonetheless be in a de facto relationship in the sense required by s 4AA.
...
... physical cohabitation at a single home or homes is not a necessary feature of an ongoing relationship whether by way of marriage or otherwise; it is not an irreducible minimum that all relationships must exhibit.

...
The language of s 4AA of the Act and its reference to “living together” requires no different approach to determining whether a relationship exists of the kind defined. “Living together”, consistently with authority, should be construed as meaning sharing life as a couple. Section 4AA does not prescribe any way by which a couple may share life together. Its language is sufficiently broad to accommodate the great variety of ways a de facto relationship may exist. That conclusion is supported by the varied factors listed in s 4AA(2). In a given case, some of the factors listed in s 4AA(2) may be relevant and some may be irrelevant; inevitably some may have greater prominence than others. A conclusion that a de facto relationship has ended may also arise because of factors not listed in s 4AA(2). Such a conclusion is mandated by s 4AA(3) and (4). In particular, s 4AA(4) is a statutory recognition that what may constitute a genuine de facto relationship is not be determined in the same way in every case by reference to rigid criteria that must always be satisfied. In that respect, the language of s 4AA(2)(b) does not assume that every de facto relationship must have a “common residence” to some “extent” and of some “nature”. Such a construction is entirely denied by s 4AA(3).

(Footnotes omitted, emphasis original)
The Full Court observed in Shelby & Rylan [2022] FedCFamC1A 143 at [19]:
The High Court has arguably clarified that parties do not need to live together in order to be in a de facto relationship if they are sharing life as a couple. This however does not mean that every couple sharing life is necessarily living in a de facto relationship. The Court must have regard to all of the evidence, including to the matters referred to in s 4AA(2) of the Act. On one view, the phrase “sharing life” is simply a general summary of the s 4AA(2) circumstances.
I have taken account of the factual matters set out above at [10]–[68].
I turn to consider the factors set forth in s 4AA(2).
It is common ground that the parties were in some form of relationship that commenced sometime in 1998 and broke down around October 2023, being a duration of about 25 years. As mentioned earlier, the parties disputed the month of commencement, being April or May according to the applicant, or December according to the respondent. The applicant claimed the relationship became de facto within weeks, primarily because the respondent introduced him to his family and gave him a key to the Suburb C property. This is not plausible. The discussion at [10]–[68] shows the progress of the relationship. The evidence does not establish the parties were living together as a couple on a genuine domestic basis in 1998 or 1999.
But there is no doubt the parties had a relationship for well in excess of two years. The central question is whether the evidence has established a de facto relationship, not some other type of relationship, came into existence lasting for a period of at least two years during that time.
The relationship in question here lasted for over two decades on either party’s evidence. This is a considerable period. The submissions of the applicant understandably emphasised the duration, because it is a striking feature of the factual matrix. However, duration is one matter among many to be taken into account. It is quite conceivable that parties may continue a relationship for many years without it ever becoming a relationship as a couple living together on a genuine domestic basis. As will be explained in the following discussion, I have concluded that is the position in this matter.
It was also common ground that a sexual relationship existed from at least 1998 until separation in 2023. But it also continued after October 2023. It was the evidence of the applicant that the parties maintained a sexual relationship until about March or May 2024.
The respondent claimed the relationship was never, and never intended to be, exclusive. The applicant denied this, though he gave evidence the parties did not discuss being exclusive, but it was “assumed” (applicant’s reply affidavit filed 15 December 2025, paragraph 15). However, the applicant need not establish sexual or other exclusivity (Rooks & Padley at [10]), although it is a factor to be weighed with all other considerations. The applicant for his part gave evidence of a holiday taken by the respondent with another man in Country EE, which caused him great distress. However, the respondent denied the sexual encounters alleged by the applicant ever happened, as mentioned I accept it is more likely than not. I find it is likely that the parties were in a largely exclusive relationship from about 1998 until October 2023.
There was a significant dispute about the existence of a common residence. The concept of a “common residence” or “household” has been the subject of much judicial discussion which can also bear upon the ambit of “living together” in s 4AA. It has long been held that there need not be “full time” living together (Moby v Schulter [2010] FamCA 748; (2010) FLC 93-447 at [140]). In Yesilhat v Calokerinos (2021) 21 ASTLR 227, Brereton JA at [125], [133] and [134] (Bathurst CJ at [1] agreeing) made some apposite comments:
While there are differences between the statutory definitions of a de facto relationship and a close personal relationship, and the notion of a common household, each of them involves a requirement that the person be “living with” the other person...
...
It is also inherent in the concept of membership of the same household that the members of the household be living together. The notion of membership of a household involves a common residence in which two or more persons not only share the facilities but also live together. Membership of (as distinct from presence in) a household involves aspects of continuity and permanency of mutual living. Although the question as to when a living arrangement becomes a household is one of degree, and there are no hard and fast rules, at least generally, the minimum requirements include at least some element of residing (as distinct from visiting), and mutual living so that there is a quasi-family unit (as distinct from boarding). The fact that a person lives in the residence of another only periodically, or for a very short period, is insufficient to render him or her a member of the other’s household, as the required degree of continuity and permanency is not present. Where one person occasionally stays over at the other’s house, but maintains his or her own separate accommodation as a principal place of residence, it cannot be said that they are living together or members of the same household, notwithstanding that the emotional relationship between them is substantial, strong, loving, intense, and enduring. In this respect, there is a distinction between a visitor and a resident, which may be easier to recognise than precisely describe; but residence involves aspects of continuity and permanence, and indicia such as use of the place as an address, and as a location where personal items and effects are kept and the activities of daily living (such as sleeping, washing and dressing) are performed.
Each of the three relevant categories of eligibility therefore involves “living together”, a concept which involves mutual living in a common residence, at least to some extent, though not necessarily exclusively or on a full-time basis. It is not essential to a finding of a de facto relationship that there be unbroken common residence, and it is not incompatible with the existence of a de facto relationship, as defined, that a party spend some nights each week elsewhere than in the “matrimonial home”. This is supported, in the case of a de facto relationship, by Interpretation Act, s 21C(3)(b), which in referring to “(b) the nature and extent of their common residence”, acknowledges that its nature and extent may vary. Many authorities, some of which are referred to below, acknowledge that continuous full-time cohabitation is not essential, although the reason for the absences will be relevant: thus absence for work travel, or leisure, will rarely if ever be inconsistent with “living together”, whereas absence because the person is maintaining another household might well be so, even if not invariably. However, some element of common residence is indispensable. As will appear, no authority holds that persons can “live together” or be members of the same household if they do not, to some extent, have a common residence.

(Footnotes omitted)
The applicant claimed he used the respondent’s homes in Suburb C, and Suburb J until its sale in 2010, as if they were his shared residences.
Apart from the Suburb C property, there was no dispute the applicant resided at the following locations throughout the relationship:
(a) From 1998 to July 2002, at student accommodation at OO College and G University;

(b) From July 2002 to 11 June 2009, with his brother at rental accommodation in Suburb L; and

(c) From 11 June 2009 to the date of trial at the Suburb U property.

There was no claim by the applicant that the respondent ever shared the Suburb L property or the Suburb U property with him as a common residence. The applicant did not give any credible evidence of living at the Suburb J property. The only possible common residence was therefore the Suburb C property. There was no dispute this was and is the residence of the respondent.
As mentioned earlier, the applicant claimed from 1998 to 2009 he spent four to six nights per week at the Suburb C property. The respondent claimed between 1998 and 2002 it was no more than two nights per week, and not every week, followed by an increase to three nights per week between 2003 to 2005. I prefer the evidence of the respondent in this regard.
From 2009 to 2023 it was agreed the applicant spent four nights per week at the Suburb C property being Wednesday, and Friday through to Sunday. As mentioned, I preferred the respondent’s evidence that it was no more than four nights and at times the applicant only spent about one night per week at the Suburb C property if the parties went away on the weekend.
The applicant gave oral evidence that between 1998 to 2012 he considered the Suburb C property his primary residence. His subjective perceptions are not determinative, but form part of the composite picture of the relationship, and if accepted as true by the Court, inform the objective assessment of all the circumstances.
But, as recorded above, the applicant never, in 25 years, gave the Suburb C property as his address when providing information to the Australian Taxation Office, the federal Department of Immigration, Services NSW to renew his driver’s licence, or the NSW Department of Education for a Working With Children Check. Instead, the applicant held out the Suburb L property, and later the Suburb U property as his residential address at all times, including for correspondence from his superannuation fund and banks.
I note that in his affidavit in reply the applicant asserted he used the Suburb C property as his address on a curriculum vitae when applying for jobs in the Sydney area (applicant’s affidavit in reply filed 15 December 2025, paragraph 17; applicant’s tender bundle, p.111). From the face of the document, it must have been brought into existence after 16 October 2020. The document is undated and the applicant gives no evidence of when it was brought into existence, or where he stored copies prior to the commencement of the proceedings. There is little mention in his trial affidavit of applying for jobs in the Sydney area besides a reference to applying for a part time casual teaching job at FF School in 2020. He gave no detail in his affidavit in reply about any dates or any particular job application for which this document was used. Although the applicant was not cross examined specifically about this document, I consider it to be unpersuasive evidence of the applicant ever presenting the Suburb C property as his address.
The applicant sought to explain the inconsistency between his asserted subjective perception of the nature of the relationship and the Suburb C property as the parties’ “forever home” and the consistent manner in which he presented his primary address as the Suburb U property to a range of federal and state regulatory authorities. One explanation was that because he lived in two homes, he wanted his mail directed to one mailbox separate from the tenants’ mail at the Suburb C property. This was unconvincing because, as he conceded, this did not explain the documents completed by him and lodged prior to 2012 when tenants did not yet occupy the Suburb C property. Another explanation was that he did not disclose the Suburb C property address because he was not openly gay and wanted to keep a separate residential address so his parents, and the wider world would not know he lived in two homes, one of which involved a gay relationship. In cross examination he claimed, for example, “I feel like I need to be safe, and guarded myself, in terms of who I told people, because I would not just willy-nilly tell everyone, random strangers, that I’m gay” (Transcript 14 January 2026, p.2 lines 45–47). Yet he admitted in cross examination that by 2020, there was no reason to conceal his residential address for this reason because he was no longer embarrassed about friends knowing he was in a same sex relationship. Nonetheless he continued to present the Suburb U property as his address in his Working with Children Check application dated September 2020.
For the same reason, namely, hiding his sexuality, he claimed he did not check the “de facto” box on his immigration form in 2002, despite claiming he considered the parties were already de facto by that time. Yet he admitted he also did not assert a de facto relationship status in his 2015 tax return, 13 years later, even though by 2015 he claimed to be in a loving committed relationship with the respondent and his friends and family knew he was gay.
While it is plausible the applicant wished to suppress wide knowledge of a gay relationship in the early years, this does not adequately explain why he consistently for the entire duration, 25 years, both failed to promote the Suburb C property as his address and denied a de facto relationship in his dealings with regulatory authorities, in documents which required him to declare the veracity of his information. Such misrepresentations made by parties to third parties about the status or characterisation of their relationship is not dispositive (Nelson v Nelson (1995) 184 CLR 538 at 568–571 and 613–617). But they undermine the credibility of the applicant’s asserted perception of the nature of the relationship, and, on the contrary, involve an open acknowledgement by him that no de facto relationship existed. They also undermine the credibility of his assertion that he lived between two homes, or shared the Suburb C property as a common residence.
The respondent’s evidence persuades me that he never viewed the Suburb C property as a common residence of the parties or welcomed the applicant into the Suburb C property on the basis of a merged life together. There is no doubt the applicant shared the Suburb C property on a regular basis by staying overnight during the relationship. But the corollary is that the applicant’s absence from the Suburb C property at other times was because he maintained his home at the Suburb U property. I accept the applicant probably kept some clothing and personal grooming items at the Suburb C property over a number of years. I accept it is likely that over a period of 25 years the applicant on occasion did washing, cooking and even some cleaning. I accept that some patterns of behaviour such as taking breakfast together at the Suburb C property on weekends remained consistent over the bulk of the relationship. These are elements of a degree of modest, fractional domesticity, but not a merger of two lives.
I also accept the applicant likely helped the respondent with some manual labour during the period when the Suburb C property was renovated. But bearing in mind the extensive nature of the renovation and its myriad other aspects, with construction over two years, detailed by the respondent in evidence which I accept, even taking the applicant’s asserted contributions at their highest, they were minor, and demonstrate the limited degree to which the parties, by 2010 to 2012 merged their lives and the degree to which neither party treated the Suburb C property as one of the applicant’s “homes”, as opposed to a place he visited regularly, and as opposed to the home of the respondent. The limited involvement of the applicant in the renovation of the Suburb C property is more consistent with a close friendship than a merger and sharing of two lives consistent with a de facto relationship.
According to the applicant, he had explained his same sex relationship to his brother and mother by 2012. However, despite the renovation of the Suburb C property the applicant did not spend time there with any greater frequency than he had previously. He did not purport to “move in” with the respondent, and the respondent denied any greater connection with the applicant after 2012, which I accept. The evidence of the applicant is that his presence in the Suburb C property and the patterns of activities there objectively remained unchanged.
Neither separately nor in combination do these factors demonstrate that the Suburb C property was at any time one home of the applicant. The evidence of the respondent satisfies me that the Suburb C property was treated by both parties as his home, where the applicant visited for material periods of time, but who maintained his own residence and separate part of his life, at the Suburb U property.
I find that the parties can only be said to have had a common residence at the Suburb C property to a limited extent, namely, the applicant was a regular visitor in the respondent’s home, as a close personal friend, and sexual partner. While there were elements of domesticity when the applicant was present, I am unable to find the parties were maintaining a common residence at the Suburb C property, except to that very limited sense.
It is clear that between 1998 to 2005, the applicant had some financial dependence on the respondent. The applicant claimed he was unemployed until 2003 when he started casual work as in the fast-food industry working about one day each week. However, he conceded he was also financially dependent on his parents, who provided him with money for living expenses and for rent. He agreed he continued being financially reliant on the respondent until 2005, when he began full time employment with Q Company and earned a “reasonable income”. He did not reimburse the respondent for expenses paid on his behalf to this point.
From 2005, his evidence was that he “became to a greater extent financially self supporting and relied less on [the respondent] to meet expenses for me” (applicant’s affidavit filed 24 November 2025, paragraph 58). He remained employed with Q Company from 2005 to 2017. In this period he claimed he paid various expenses for the respondent, but under cross examination it appeared he actually paid his share, and was mostly, if not invariably reimbursed by the respondent for any expenditure he made on behalf of both parties, such as travel expenses and down to even $8 movie tickets.
The applicant made the oral submission that the parties' process of reimbursement, effectively each to pay their own way, constituted “interdependence” because it demonstrated a level of financial connectivity which is cooperative and requires and depends upon the other to participate in this approach that they took to sharing expenses. In my view, this submission confuses the manner in which funds were provided to meet expenses with a relationship where one party depends upon the other to provide the funds. The former is the situation here and is not an example of interdependence as opposed to co-operation.
Financial independence is not uncommon in modern relationships and not of itself inconsistent with life as a couple on a genuine domestic basis (Delamarre & Asprey [2014] FamCAFC 218 at [19]). But I take account of the fact that, apart from the early period when the respondent was more financially supportive, the parties remained financially separate for over 20 years. They did not merge their patterns of expenditure, other than to the limited degree already described.
There is no jointly owned property or joint bank accounts. There was no evidence the parties even contemplated acquiring joint property during the 25 years.
The applicant did not involve the respondent in the purchase of the Suburb U property. He argued the parties did not purchase property together because their 28 year age gap left them in different life stages. He argued the respondent had “mostly completed the property acquisition phase in his life prior to meeting the Applicant” (applicant’s Case Outline filed 5 January 2026, paragraph 41) and that the applicant was not in a position to buy property. Even if so, the applicant at least in 2009, when he was financially able to invest in property, made a choice not to include the person he claimed to be his partner of about 10 years at that point in the investment.
It may also be said that the very fact of the parties being at “different life stages” is one reason why they never merged their lives to the extent consistent with a de facto relationship.
The applicant submitted that although the parties did not purchase property together, they “dealt with their property in parallel” and “suitable to their circumstances”, with reference to the respondent’s transition to retirement (applicant’s Case Outline filed 5 January 2026, paragraph 40). He submitted that he provided labour for renovations to the Suburb C property to help convert the respondent’s existing property into an income-generating asset to support retirement. I reject this argument, and refer to my finding that the applicant’s contributions in relation to the renovation were minor.
It was undisputed the parties spent a lot of time together on the respondent’s boat. The applicant claimed that he helped maintain the boat, for example doing antifouling maintenance, and polishing the boat's teak decking plus engine maintenance. I am not convinced by this evidence. It was clear from his cross examination that the applicant conceded polishing the teak deck did not happen and antifouling had to be done by an expert.
The public aspects of the relationship are equivocal.
A number of witnesses were called by both parties going to the public presentation of the relationship, each saying the evidence supported their case. But the impressions held by these witnesses of the parties socially was of little help in determining if there was a de facto relationship or a another type of close relationship which did not fall within s 4AA. None actually stated unequivocally that they thought the parties were in a relationship which was “de facto”, as opposed to conceding they made assumptions about the relationship. This is equivocal at best.
Within the gay community, the applicant did not conceal the relationship because he considered it to be a safe environment. The parties had a number of mutual friends within the community. They regularly attended the same social activities, such as lunches, dinners, and event in Region Z, but disagreed if they presented publicly as a couple at these events. For the event in Region Z there was no requirement that attendees be in a relationship. The parties often arrived and departed from the events together, though not on every occasion. The applicant tendered a number of photographs of the parties in group settings at balls, bushwalks and dinners, but this evidence was equivocal about the nature of the relationship. The respondent denied the parties showed affection at these occasions or that the parties even spent much time together at the events. However, some of the photos indicate a level of affection between the parties.
The applicant claimed the respondent on occasion referred to him as the respondent’s “partner” over the course of the relationship. The respondent denied ever doing so. I generally prefer the evidence of the respondent. But even if he did refer to the applicant as his “partner” occasionally over 25 years, this infrequent and random usage of itself suggests a level of disinterest in acknowledging the applicant in that way.
It was not contentious that the applicant spent time with the respondent's family beyond celebrating special occasions. The parties disagreed when the applicant first met the respondent's family, either in May 1998 or December 1999. The applicant claimed the respondent was immediately open with his family about the parties being in a relationship, and he felt included in their family circle. The respondent denied the applicant was considered part of his family and said his family referred to the applicant as his “friend”, never as his “partner”.
The applicant claimed his mother developed a strong relationship with the respondent after meeting him in 2013. The respondent in cross examination referred to the applicant’s mother as his “friend”, agreed that he knew her very well, and met her on numerous occasions. But he did not consider himself part of the applicant’s family circle as he was not accepted by Mr M, the applicant’s brother. I accept this was a genuinely held view.
One indication of a mutual commitment to a shared life is taking the step of marriage. This was open to the parties after amendments to the Marriage Act 1961 (Cth) in 2017. It is a statement of public nature about mutual commitment. However, the parties never married, and according to the evidence never discussed getting married.

Conclusion

The applicant submitted the parties had enmeshed their lives over 25 years in a relationship characterised by “reciprocity, shared responsibility and reliability” (applicant’s Case Outline filed 5 January 2026, paragraph 47). He submitted there was a “meeting of the minds” of a mutual commitment to a shared life, evidenced by the parties’ behaviour throughout the relationship. The applicant pointed out the parties regularly spent social occasions, such as group lunches and dinners, Christmas and birthday events together year after year, and regularly went on holidays. He contended the parties presented as a couple when attending social activities with their various clubs.
In relation to the respondent’s practice of reimbursing the applicant for expenses, he argued that the parties’ “coordinated approach to expenses evidences a relationship characterised by stability and shared goals” (applicant’s Case Outline filed 5 January 2026, paragraph 51). The applicant contended this behaviour over 25 years demonstrated the parties’ mutual commitment to shared experiences and social groups, and any financial independence does not diminish the reality of their integrated lives.
The respondent denied there was any degree of mutual commitment to a shared life. He asserted it was a close personal friendship maintained over 25 years without contemplation of marriage, living together, or formalisation of the relationship. He claimed the parties did not attend a number of significant events together such as the applicant’s graduation or work functions. He denied they offered emotional support to each other, even in times of emotional distress. He gave evidence that he imposed no expectations on the applicant, such as exclusivity, and was not aware of his activities much of the time.
I reject the applicant’s arguments and his ultimate contention that the parties had a relationship as a couple living together on a genuine domestic basis. I accept the parties over many years attended social events, and bushwalking groups, often as a pair, which appeared at times to mark them as a couple. I also accept that on occasion they interacted in a warm and quietly affectionate way in the presence of family or friends. They also shared Christmas and Easter throughout the relationship.
But taking account of the discussion of all the matters above at [87]–[141], I am unable to accept the Suburb C property was the parties’ “forever home” or a shared residence. I have already referred to the fact that the applicant never claimed the Suburb C property as his residential address, and failed to nominate his relationship status as “de facto”, in documents submitted to governmental and revenue authorities. As mentioned, the parties maintained separate personal finances, and the respondent undertook a substantial renovation of the Suburb C property with minimal involvement of the applicant. Overall, I do not entirely accept the evidence of the applicant about his domestic contributions at the Suburb C property, such as cooking, washing and cleaning. I do not accept his evidence about providing care to the respondent’s mother at the Suburb C property.
I find that the parties lacked a mutual commitment to a shared life. The level of sharing was limited and I am not satisfied the parties held the same commitment to the relationship, which undermines the existence of a necessary level of mutuality. The applicant was likely more emotionally dependent upon the respondent, who, on the other hand, was comfortable maintaining a higher degree of separateness between the parties, despite a level of affection (Cizek & Mihov [2024] FedCFamC1A 151 at [9], [36]). I accept the evidence of the respondent that he at no time had any expectations of the applicant, nor required exclusivity. I accept there was a level of consistency and regularity in the pattern of the parties’ interactions and shared experiences, but this did not ever reach the level of a merger of two lives. Objectively the relationship cannot be meaningfully distinguished from a long term close friendship involving sex.
A further factual matter which supports this conclusion is the way the parties conducted themselves after the applicant claimed the relationship had broken down. A finding that a de facto relationship has broken down is not precluded by the presence of an ongoing relationship of some sort (Fairbairn at [29]). Here the existence of an ongoing relationship is a matter which militates against finding a de facto relationship existed at any point in time. Despite the applicant asserting the de facto relationship ended in October 2023, he claimed he continued to visit and engage in sexual intercourse with the respondent at the Suburb C property on occasion for about six months. The parties spent Christmas 2023 together and attended group bushwalks in March 2024 appearing as a couple, though he agreed they were not. The relationship that continued between the parties after October 2023 is relevant, in the sense that, as the respondent submitted, there was objectively no material difference in how the parties conducted themselves and spent time together before and after the date of separation promoted by the applicant. In other words, there was no persuasive basis in the evidence to distinguish the nature of the relationship between the period when the applicant asserted there existed a de facto relationship and the period when, even on his case, there did not.
Taking all of the above matters together, even on an impressionistic evaluation of the facts generous to the applicant, I cannot find the parties ever had a relationship as a couple living together on a genuine domestic basis, and therefore the criterion in s 4AA(1)(c) is not satisfied.
Accordingly, the Court’s jurisdiction under Pt VIIIAB is not attracted and the applicant’s application must be dismissed. It also follows that the declaration sought by the respondent that a de facto relationship never existed between the parties should be made.
While strictly unnecessary, in the event the matter is taken further, it is appropriate to express the conclusion I would have reached if the Court’s jurisdiction had been attracted, at least on the question of whether it would be just and equitable to disturb the existing property interests.

PART VIIIAB

Part VIIIAB of the Act sets out the legislative provisions relating to property orders that may be sought when parties were in a de facto relationship. The central provision is s 90SM of the Act, which gives the Court power to make such orders for alteration of property interests as it considers appropriate.
These proceedings were heard after 10 June 2025, so the amendments to the Act brought into effect by the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) apply.
Section 90SM(1) empowers the Court to make such orders as it considers appropriate in “altering the interests of the parties to the de facto relationship” in “the property of the parties to the de facto relationship or either of them”.
Section 90SM(2) of the Act provides that:
The court must not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.
In Stanford v Stanford [2012] FamCAFC 1; (2012) 247 CLR 108 (“Stanford”) the High Court commented at [36] on the meaning of “just and equitable” in the equivalent s 79(2) as follows:
The expression "just and equitable" is a qualitative description of a conclusion reached after examination of a range of potentially competing considerations. It does not admit of exhaustive definition. It is not possible to chart its metes and bounds...
(Footnotes omitted)
Stanford at [40] also made clear that “[t]o conclude that making an order is ‘just and equitable’ only because of and by reference to various matters in [s 90SM(4) and s 90SM(5)], without a separate consideration of [s 90SM(2)] would be to conflate the statutory requirements and ignore the principles laid down by the Act” (emphasis original). The requirement to make an order that is just and equitable permeates the entire decision-making process and is not a threshold issue (Martin & Newton [2011] FamCAFC 233; (2011) FLC 93-490 at [306]; Bevan & Bevan [2013] FamCAFC 116; (2013) FLC 93-545 (“Bevan”) at [62], [86]).
If the Court is satisfied that it is just and equitable to make a property adjustment order, the Court is obliged to consider the matters set forth in s 90SM(4) and s 90SM(5).
The Court is required to make orders which are not only “just and equitable” but also “appropriate” (Zao & Lee [2019] FamCAFC 169 at [48]; Aitken & Aitken [2023] FedCFamC1A 69; (2023) FLC 94-142 at [59]). Section 90SS grants specific powers to make a range of different orders to adjust property interests.
These judicial statements and the approach they articulate apply with equal force to an application for determination under Pt VIIIAB of the Act, particularly s 90SM in relation to a de facto relationship.
