IS ANY ORDER JUST AND EQUITABLE?

I consider it appropriate to explain my reasons concerning the determination required by s 90SM(2) at this point, which I would have made if the Court’s jurisdiction was attracted, contrary to my conclusion above.
The considerations set forth in s 90SM(4) and s 90SM(5) of the Act may inform both the exercise of the discretion in deciding whether it is just, equitable and proper to make an order and then in evaluating what that order should be, but it is not mandatory to consider them for this purpose (Cosola & Moretto [2023] FedCFamC1A 61; (2023) 66 Fam LR 480 at [41]–[43]; Costello & Langdon [2024] FedCFamC1A 168; (2024) FLC 94-212 at [38]). I have considered them only to the extent explained in the following discussion.
For the reasons which follow, I am not satisfied, after taking account of the following matters, which, I observe, would also fall for consideration in s 90SM(4) or s 90SM(5), it would be just and equitable to make any order adjusting the parties’ existing property interests in this matter.
In doing so, several fundamental points should preface the discussion. The question of whether it is just and equitable to make an order “is not to be answered by assuming that the parties’ rights or interests in marital property are or should be different” from those at the time when the discretion may be exercised (Stanford at [37]–[39], [50]; Wirth v Wirth [1956] HCA 71; (1956) 98 CLR 228 at 231–232 and 247–248). There is no presumption of equal sharing of property interests or community property in Australian law (Mallet v Mallet [1984] HCA 21; (1984) 156 CLR 605 (“Mallet”)). Unless and until property rights or interests are adjusted pursuant to s 79 or s 90SM, one spouse has no inchoate or other proprietary interest in the exclusive property owned by the other spouse (Bevan at [80]; Lin & Ruan [2021] FamCAFC 90; (2021) FLC 94-024 at [41], [48]–[49]; Sarto & Sarto [2022] FedCFamC1A 16; (2022) 65 Fam LR 605 (“Sarto”) at [19]; Agnarsson & Agnarsson (No 4) [2024] FedCFamC1F 407 at [46]; B Pty Ltd & Anker [2025] FedCFamC1A 85; (2025) 71 Fam LR 358 at [16]). It is wrong to assume that one spouse enjoys an inchoate interest in the exclusive property of the other spouse “simply because it was acquired during the marriage” (Sarto at [24]). Until the Court exercises its discretion to adjust property interests and unless restrained by an injunction made under s 114 of the Act, the spouses may each exert the full measure of their property rights against third parties and one another (Kartal & Templeman [2022] FedCFamC1A 46 at [53]).
A large disparity in the financial positions of the parties, of itself, does not justify alteration of the property of the parties; the objective is not to equalise the financial strengths of the parties, but to effect a redistribution of the parties’ property if it be just and equitable to do so (Mallet per Wilson J at 638; Clauson and Clauson [1995] FamCA 10; (1995) FLC 92-595 at 81,912).
To repeat, the parties kept their financial affairs almost entirely separate, never owned property jointly, never held joint bank accounts, and paid their own way individually or by occasional reimbursement for outings and living expenses such as food.
The applicant is 46 years of age, and has good health. He has cash savings of at least $104,885, shares worth $27,534 and superannuation of $150,155. He owns, and has owned for many years, a property to live in, at Suburb U, with his brother which is unencumbered. He has no liabilities of any significance. The applicant presently claims weekly income of $223 or about $11,500 per annum. However, he gave no clear evidence about his capacity for future employment, although his age and employment history up to trial permits the inference that he has capacity for gainful employment. This was conceded in his Case Outline filed 5 January 2026 and at final submissions. His curriculum vitae and tax returns for the financial years 2017 to 2025 show a capacity to earn a much larger income and his age shows he should have at least several decades of earning capacity ahead of him.
The respondent is 74 years of age and retired. He has good health. He has modest superannuation of $11,538. He owns the Suburb C property unencumbered, worth $6,850,000, and his boat which has a value of $92,500. He has owned the Suburb C property since 1992, many years before meeting the applicant. Its current value is at least partially a result of the time and money he spent in renovating it in 2010 to 2012. The property is his only source of income, through renting rooms to lodgers.
As mentioned, the applicant claims an adjustment of property interests dividing the available property 40 per cent to him. Any orders altering the property interests to award the applicant additional property beyond his existing interests would require the respondent to either sell the Suburb C property or borrow money then service the loan, for example, by drawing upon an existing line of credit (CBA #83). Indeed, the applicant points to this resource as demonstrating a capacity to meet any order for cash adjustment in his favour.
The applicant also pointed to the likelihood that the respondent would receive support and an inheritance from his mother who will turn 100 years of age in March 2026 and owns a property in Suburb QQ. However, while the respondent’s mother is alive, this remains only a possible source of further financial means, and the respondent’s mother was clear in her evidence that she will not sell her property during her lifetime because this would preclude her from receiving the aged pension.
In my view, the matters referred to above at [166]–[172] would constitute a separate and sufficient basis to refuse any adjustment to the parties’ property. They rule out satisfaction that any order adjusting property would be just and equitable. I am not persuaded it would be just and equitable to force the respondent to either sell the Suburb C property, or incur substantial debt obligations at his age, when the applicant has property, material superannuation, no liabilities, and a capacity for many more years of employment. These matters would be sufficient reason to refuse the application for property adjustment.
For these reasons I would not be satisfied it is just and equitable to make any orders disturbing the existing property of the parties.
It is also inappropriate, for the same reasons, to make the declaration sought by the respondent that the applicant did not make contributions of the kind contained in ss 90SM(4)(a), 90SM(4)(b) and 90SM(4)(c).

CONCLUSION

For all the foregoing reasons the application for property adjustment orders should be dismissed.

COSTS

I will order that any party who seeks costs to file the relevant application within 28 days of these orders.
